county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Adams County formed 1902 from Arapahoe County. County seat is Brighton. Named for Governor Alva Adams. Part of the Denver metropolitan area; historically agricultural (sugar beets, livestock) with suburban growth in the late 20th century. Commerce City and Thornton are major communities.
county_metadata.research_tips: Adams County Clerk and Recorder in Brighton holds deeds, mortgages, and marriage licenses from 1902. Created from Arapahoe County -- check Arapahoe County for pre-1902 records. The county was part of Denver's suburban growth corridor and attracted immigrant sugar beet workers (German-Russian, Hispanic, Japanese) in the early 1900s. Brighton and Commerce City historical societies have local collections. Colorado State Archives has microfilmed county records. BLM GLO land patents for Adams County homesteads are searchable at glorecords.blm.gov. FamilySearch has indexed Colorado vital records that include Adams County marriages and some death records. Denver Public Library Western History department covers the broader metro area including Adams County.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Adams County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Alamosa County formed 1913 from Costilla and Conejos counties. County seat is Alamosa. Located in the San Luis Valley with deep Hispanic heritage. Adams State University. Denver & Rio Grande Railroad hub. Agricultural economy (potatoes, barley, livestock).
county_metadata.research_tips: Alamosa County Clerk and Recorder holds records from 1913 when the county was carved from Costilla and Conejos counties -- search both parent counties for earlier records. The San Luis Valley has deep Hispanic heritage dating to the 1850s, with many families tracing to original Mexican and New Mexican land grants. Catholic parish records at Our Lady of the Valley and Sacred Heart churches document Hispanic families from the area's settlement. Adams State University in Alamosa holds regional historical collections including oral histories of San Luis Valley families. The Colorado State Archives and History Colorado have San Luis Valley materials. Alamosa was a Denver & Rio Grande Railroad hub -- check railroad employment records at the Colorado Railroad Museum in Golden.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Alamosa County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Arapahoe County formed 1861 as one of the original 17 territorial counties. County seat moved to Littleton in 1904 after Denver separated. Named for the Arapaho people. Originally the largest county in Colorado Territory, parent to many later counties.
county_metadata.research_tips: Arapahoe County Clerk and Recorder in Littleton holds records from 1861 as one of the original 17 territorial counties. The original Arapahoe County was enormous, covering most of eastern Colorado Territory -- many later counties were carved from it (including Denver in 1902 and Adams in 1902). Early mining camp and settlement records overlap with Denver. Colorado State Archives holds early territorial records. Arapahoe County District Court records in the state archives include probate, civil, and criminal cases from the 1860s. The Littleton Museum and Englewood Public Library have local history collections. BLM GLO patents cover homesteading claims. Cherry Creek settlements predate the formal county organization -- check early Denver-area sources for 1858-1861 gold rush migrants.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Arapahoe County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Archuleta County formed 1885 from Conejos County. County seat is Pagosa Springs. Named for the Archuleta family prominent in southern Colorado and New Mexico. Ute heritage; Spanish colonial settlement.
county_metadata.research_tips: Archuleta County Clerk and Recorder in Pagosa Springs holds records from 1885. Created from Conejos County -- check Conejos for earlier records. The county has significant Ute Indian history (the Pagosa Springs area was Ute homeland). Spanish and Mexican heritage is strong; some land claims trace to colonial-era grants. Archuleta County has a small population and limited local repositories -- Colorado State Archives and History Colorado in Denver are the primary research centers. Catholic parish records in Pagosa Springs serve the Hispanic community. The Southern Ute Indian Reservation borders Archuleta County; tribal records may be relevant for families with Ute connections. BLM GLO records document federal land patents in the area.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Archuleta County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Baca County formed 1889 from Las Animas County. County seat is Springfield. Named for pioneer Felipe Baca. Remote southeastern plains county. Severely affected by the 1930s Dust Bowl. Comanchero and Santa Fe Trail heritage.
county_metadata.research_tips: Baca County Clerk and Recorder in Springfield holds records from 1889 (created from Las Animas). Remote southeastern plains county -- one of the hardest-hit areas during the 1930s Dust Bowl, which caused massive population displacement. Many families left during the 1930s, so researchers should check destination states (California, Oregon, Washington) for continuing records. Baca County Museum in Springfield has local collections. The county was part of the Comanche territory and later the Santa Fe Trail corridor. Homestead records at BLM GLO are essential for tracking original settlers. Colorado State Archives has county records on microfilm. Catholic and Protestant church records in Springfield document families from the late 1800s.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Baca County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Bent County formed 1870 from unorganized territory. County seat is Las Animas (the town). Named for the Bent brothers (William and Charles) who built Bent's Fort on the Santa Fe Trail. Sand Creek Massacre (1864) heritage. Sugar beet farming.
county_metadata.research_tips: Bent County Clerk and Recorder in Las Animas (the town) holds records from 1870. Named for Bent's Fort, the famous fur trading post on the Santa Fe Trail (now Bent's Old Fort National Historic Site). The county has layered heritage: Native American (Cheyenne, Arapaho, Kiowa, Comanche), Hispanic (Mexican-era settlement), and Anglo-American (Santa Fe Trail traders, then farmers). Fort Lyon (established 1860) records are at NARA -- military post records document soldiers, families, and nearby residents. The Sand Creek Massacre (1864) occurred in what was then part of this area. Sugar beet farming brought German-Russian, Hispanic, and Japanese laborers in the early 1900s. Colorado State Archives has microfilmed Bent County records. The Bent County Democrat newspaper (from 1887) is a valuable source for obituaries and legal notices.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Bent County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Boulder County formed 1861 as one of the original 17 territorial counties. County seat is Boulder. University of Colorado (1876). Gold and coal mining heritage. Agricultural and academic community that became a technology center.
county_metadata.research_tips: Boulder County Clerk and Recorder holds records from 1861 as an original territorial county. The University of Colorado Boulder's Norlin Library Special Collections and Archives holds extensive regional history materials including mining records, labor history, and university-related family papers. The Carnegie Branch Library for Local History in Boulder is an outstanding genealogical resource with indexed obituaries, city directories (from 1890s), photograph collections, and oral histories. Gold Hill, Caribou, and other mining camps have records scattered among state archives and local collections. Boulder County was a center of coal mining -- check mine records and labor union records. Colorado State Archives holds Boulder County vital records and court records on microfilm. FamilySearch has digitized many Boulder County records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Boulder County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Broomfield County formed 2001 from Adams, Boulder, Jefferson, and Weld counties -- the newest county in Colorado and the 64th. Previously part of four different counties. Suburban Denver community. Rocky Flats nuclear weapons plant nearby.
county_metadata.research_tips: Broomfield County was created in 2001 from parts of Adams, Boulder, Jefferson, and Weld counties -- the newest county in Colorado and one of the newest in the nation. For records before 2001, you MUST check all four parent counties. The Broomfield Depot Museum and Broomfield Veterans Memorial Museum have local history materials. Broomfield was a small farming community that grew rapidly after World War II as part of Denver's suburban expansion. The Rocky Flats nuclear weapons plant (now a wildlife refuge) employed many Broomfield-area families -- employee records may be at the Department of Energy. County Clerk and Recorder records begin only from 2001. Colorado State Archives and the parent county clerk offices hold all earlier records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Broomfield County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Chaffee County formed 1879 from Lake County. County seat is Salida. Named for Senator Jerome Chaffee. Upper Arkansas River valley. Mining heritage (Monarch, St. Elmo). Railroad town (Denver & Rio Grande, Denver South Park & Pacific).
county_metadata.research_tips: Chaffee County Clerk and Recorder in Salida holds records from 1879 (created from Lake County). The county encompasses the upper Arkansas River valley and was a center of mining (Monarch, St. Elmo, Romley) and railroad activity (Denver & Rio Grande, Denver South Park & Pacific). The ghost towns of St. Elmo and Hancock document mining-era families. Buena Vista and Salida were railroad towns. Colorado State Penitentiary records (Canon City is in neighboring Fremont County) may document Chaffee County residents. Salida Regional Library has local history materials. Check Lake County for pre-1879 records (original parent). BLM GLO records for mining claims and homesteads. History Colorado and Colorado State Archives hold county microfilm.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Chaffee County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Cheyenne County formed 1889 from Bent and Elbert counties. County seat is Cheyenne Wells. Named for the Cheyenne people. Eastern plains ranching and farming. Sparse population.
county_metadata.research_tips: Cheyenne County Clerk and Recorder in Cheyenne Wells holds records from 1889 (created from Bent and Elbert counties). A sparsely populated eastern plains county with ranching and farming heritage. Named for the Cheyenne people who were indigenous to the area. The county was part of the open range cattle era of the 1870s-1880s before homesteaders arrived. Dust Bowl conditions in the 1930s caused population decline. Church records (Methodist, Presbyterian, Catholic) in Cheyenne Wells and Kit Carson are important supplements. BLM GLO homestead patents document original settlers. Colorado State Archives has microfilmed county records. Check Bent and Elbert counties for pre-1889 records. The Cheyenne Wells Gazette newspaper is a source for vital records and legal notices.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Cheyenne County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.source_name: Cheyenne County Probate Records -- District Court / Colorado State Archives
source_profiles.repository: Cheyenne County District Court / Colorado State Archives, Denver
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, estates, guardianships, and probate proceedings for Cheyenne County; District Court handles probate in Colorado; older records may be at Colorado State Archives on microfilm

source_profiles.source_name: Cheyenne County Federal Land Patents -- BLM GLO Records
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents for Cheyenne County including homestead entries under the Homestead Act (1862), Timber Culture Act, and Desert Land Act; searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov; essential for tracking original settlers on the eastern plains

source_profiles.source_name: Cheyenne County Federal Census Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census for Cheyenne County from 1890; county formed after 1885 state census -- check parent county for earlier enumerations
